evals.prompt: In my webforJ app I want to refresh a stock quote panel every 5 seconds. Wire it up.
evals.expected_output: The agent uses `com.webforj.Interval` with a 5-second delay, attaches an elapsed listener that performs the refresh, calls `interval.start()` to activate it, and stops it on the view's destroy hook so the timer does not survive after navigation. No `java.util.Timer`, `javax.swing.Timer`, or raw `Thread` is introduced.
expectations: Constructs an `Interval` from `com.webforj.Interval` with the delay in seconds (e.g. `5f`)
Registers the refresh callback as an `EventListener<Interval.ElapsedEvent>` (passed to the constructor or via `addElapsedListener(...)`)
Calls `interval.start()` to activate the interval (it does NOT auto-start)
Stops the interval in the view's destroy hook (e.g. `interval.stop()` inside `onDestroy()` or `onDidDestroy()`)
Does NOT introduce `java.util.Timer`, `javax.swing.Timer`, `Executors.newScheduledThreadPool`, or `new Thread(...)` for the periodic schedule

The `Interval`'s elapsed listener body sets components directly (no `Environment.runLater` wrap, since `Interval` runs on the UI thread). This assertion fails if the listener body wraps component mutations in `Environment.runLater(...)`. It also fails (NOT vacuously) if no `Interval` was constructed at all (i.e. the agent used a banned mechanism).

evals.prompt: I have a search box that fires a server query on every keystroke. Add debouncing so the search only runs after the user stops typing for 300 ms. Make sure pending searches are cancelled if the view is destroyed.
evals.expected_output: The agent introduces a `com.webforj.Debouncer` with a 0.3f-second delay, calls `debouncer.run(() -> search(...))` from the text field's `onModify` (or equivalent change event) handler, and cancels the pending action in the view's destroy hook with `debouncer.cancel()`.
expectations: Creates a `Debouncer` from `com.webforj.Debouncer` with the delay in seconds expressed as a `float` (e.g. `0.3f`), NOT in milliseconds and NOT as an `int`
Calls `debouncer.run(() -> ...)` from inside the input event handler so each keystroke resets the timer
Calls `debouncer.cancel()` from the view's destroy hook (e.g. inside `onDidDestroy()` or `onDestroy()`)

Does NOT introduce a `java.util.Timer`, `javax.swing.Timer`, `ScheduledExecutorService`, `CompletableFuture.runAsync`, an `ExecutorService`, a raw `new Thread(...)`, or any hand-rolled timestamp-based debouncing for the debounce mechanism

Every UI component mutation in the file is reachable only from the UI thread. If the agent introduces ANY non-UI thread (CompletableFuture, ExecutorService, Thread, ScheduledExecutorService), every UI mutation in that thread's body is wrapped in `Environment.runLater(...)`. Mutating a component directly from a non-UI thread (e.g. `resultsList.add(...)` inside a `CompletableFuture.runAsync` body without `runLater`) is broken and would throw `IllegalStateException` at runtime.

evals.prompt: I have a long CPU-bound calculation that takes ~10 seconds. I need to run it without freezing the UI and update a progress bar from 0 to 100 as it runs. Cancel cleanly if the view is destroyed.
evals.expected_output: The agent runs the calculation on a managed `ExecutorService` (or `CompletableFuture.runAsync` with an explicit executor), wraps every UI update inside `Environment.runLater(...)`, captures every returned `PendingResult` so they can be cancelled, uses a `volatile boolean isCancelled` cooperative flag the worker checks each iteration, and cancels both the task and all pending UI updates in the view's destroy hook.
expectations: Wraps every UI mutation that runs on a background thread in `Environment.runLater(...)` (from `com.webforj.Environment`)
Captures the `PendingResult` returned by `Environment.runLater(...)` for at least the progress updates so they can be cancelled later
Uses a `volatile boolean isCancelled` (or equivalent) flag that the background worker checks each iteration to support cooperative cancellation
Runs the long task on a managed `ExecutorService` or `CompletableFuture.runAsync(..., executor)` started from inside the view (i.e. the worker thread inherits the `Environment` context)
Uses a single-thread daemon executor pattern (`Executors.newSingleThreadExecutor(...)` with `setDaemon(true)`) when the executor is owned by the view

On view destroy, sets the cancellation flag, cancels the running task with `cancel(true)`, cancels every tracked `PendingResult` whose `isDone()` is false, AND shuts down the executor with `executor.shutdown()`
Guards component access on the cancellation path with `if (!component.isDestroyed()) { ... }` (or equivalent) so a late-firing cancel does not write to a torn-down component
Properly handles `InterruptedException` inside the worker loop (catches it, restores the interrupt flag with `Thread.currentThread().interrupt()`, and exits the loop)
Does NOT call `progressBar.setValue(...)` or any other component method directly from the background thread (every UI mutation goes through `Environment.runLater`)
Does NOT use `java.util.Timer`, `javax.swing.Timer`, or a raw `new Thread(...)` from a static field or static initializer for the work

evals.prompt: My webforJ Spring Boot app has a service that runs a long task. The view shows a progress bar that should update from the service. Wire the async service correctly so UI updates do not throw.
evals.expected_output: The agent annotates the app class with `@EnableAsync`, marks the service method `@Async` returning `CompletableFuture<T>`, has the service accept a progress callback (`Consumer<Integer>` or similar), and the view passes a callback whose body wraps every UI update in `Environment.runLater(...)`. The view also cancels the task with `cancel(true)` on destroy.
expectations: Adds `@EnableAsync` to the Spring Boot application class (in addition to whatever annotations were already there)
Marks the service method `@Async` and returns a `CompletableFuture<T>` (NOT a raw value or `void`)
Wraps every UI update inside the progress callback AND inside `whenComplete(...)` in `Environment.runLater(...)`, with no exceptions
Stores the returned `CompletableFuture` in a view field so it can be cancelled later
On view destroy, calls `currentTask.cancel(true)` (the `true` argument interrupts the worker thread)
Does NOT touch UI components directly from the `@Async` method or the `whenComplete` callback (every UI op goes through `Environment.runLater`)
Does NOT replace the Spring async path with `java.util.Timer`, `javax.swing.Timer`, or a raw `new Thread(...)` started from the view
